coredata.dc:title: Orange peel-derived carbon dots/Cu-MOF nanohybrid for fluorescence determination of l-ascorbic acid and Fe3 +
coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghi-chahnasir, Fatemeh
Amiripour, Fatemeh
Ghasemi, Shahram
coredata.dc:description: Recycling and reuse of biomass waste in synthesis of nanomaterials have recently received much attention as an effective solution for environmental protection and sustainable development. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with blue emission were synthesized from the orange peels as a precursor through a simple hydrothermal method and then, modified with ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (N-CD@EDTA). The N-CD@EDTA was embedded as a fluorophore in Cu-based metal-organic framework (MOF-199) structure (N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199) to construct fluorescence sensor toward l-ascorbic acid (L-AA) determination. The N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 nanohybrid significantly and selectively turned on toward L-AA determination during the fluorimetric experiments. Under optimal conditions, the probe showed a suitable linear response in the concentration range of 10 nM–100 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 8.6 nM and high sensitivity of 0.201 μM−1. The possible mechanism of recognition and adsorption, including the reduction of Cu 2 + nodes in the MOF-199 structure in the presence of L-AA and the release of trapped N-CD@EDTA into the solution, was explored. Moreover, the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199/L-AA (100 μM) system was further applied as a fluorescent “on–off” sensor for Fe3 + determination with a LOD of 1.15 μM. The proposed probe was successfully used in orange juice and water samples to determine L-AA and Fe3 + with satisfactory recovery, which displays the promising capability of sensor in real samples. The recoveries obtained by suggested method are consistent with that obtained from high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and atomic absorption spectroscopy which confirm the favorable characteristic of the sensor for accurate determination of L-AA and Fe3 + in practical applications.

head.ce:title: Orange peel-derived carbon dots/Cu-MOF nanohybrid for fluorescence determination of l-ascorbic acid and Fe3 +

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Mazandaran, Babolsar, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recycling and reuse of biomass waste in synthesis of nanomaterials have recently received much attention as an effective solution for environmental protection and sustainable development. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with blue emission were synthesized from the orange peels as a precursor through a simple hydrothermal method and then, modified with ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (N-CD@EDTA). The N-CD@EDTA was embedded as a fluorophore in Cu-based metal-organic framework (MOF-199) structure (N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199) to construct fluorescence sensor toward l-ascorbic acid (L-AA) determination. The N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 nanohybrid significantly and selectively turned on toward L-AA determination during the fluorimetric experiments. Under optimal conditions, the probe showed a suitable linear response in the concentration range of 10 nM–100 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 8.6 nM and high sensitivity of 0.201 μM−1. The possible mechanism of recognition and adsorption, including the reduction of Cu 2 + nodes in the MOF-199 structure in the presence of L-AA and the release of trapped N-CD@EDTA into the solution, was explored. Moreover, the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199/L-AA (100 μM) system was further applied as a fluorescent “on–off” sensor for Fe3 + determination with a LOD of 1.15 μM. The proposed probe was successfully used in orange juice and water samples to determine L-AA and Fe3 + with satisfactory recovery, which displays the promising capability of sensor in real samples. The recoveries obtained by suggested method are consistent with that obtained from high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and atomic absorption spectroscopy which confirm the favorable characteristic of the sensor for accurate determination of L-AA and Fe3 + in practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin C, or l-ascorbic acid (L-AA), is one of the most important vitamins in the body [1] that plays a key role in the biological and physiological strategy of organisms [2,3]. L-AA is one of the most powerful antioxidants and is soluble in water [4,5] that reduces the risk of cancer, pearl, and mental illnesses [6]. It also causes the natural growth of the body tissue and cells [7]. Vitamin C cannot be produced in the human body to a proper extent, so it should be provided through the consumption of fruits, vegetables, and medicines rich in L-AA [8]. Meantime, an inappropriate amount of L-AA leads to kidney stones, infection, anemia, and other diseases [9,10], so the designing and constructing of a simple and sensitive pathway to measure L-AA are in priority [11].

Up to now, methods such as capillary electrophoresis, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and chromatography, which have high accuracy and sensitivity, were used for the detection of L-AA. However, these techniques are expensive and complex, which is a major problem of them. Recently, fluorescence techniques have attracted attention because of their simplicity, cheapness, sensitivity, and reliability [12,13].

Meanwhile, one of the most fluorescence probes, which gain great tremendous in the area of bioimaging, biosensing, and disease detection, is based on carbon dots (CDs) [14,15]. CDs are a new category of recently fluorescent carbon materials, which have not the limitations of quantum dots such as toxicity and expensively. Most CDs have a low quantum yield (QY) [16,17], in this regard, heteroatom doping is considered as a suitable method to increase the QY [18]. With surface functionalization, the inherent properties of CDs, such as optical, chemical, and electronic properties can be adjusted [19,20]. In particular, the production of a variety of CDs doped with N heteroatom is a typical tactic for the construction of a fluorescence probe with enriched electron donation [21]. Nitrogen has an atomic size comparable to carbon and also has five electrons in the valence layer that can increase the acceptance ability of CDs toward multiple targets and possible applications, therefore doping nitrogen (N-CD) can form more effective active sites onto the surface of CDs, and promoting the sensitivity of the probe [22]. But still, due to easy accumulation, instability, and low selectivity in analytical applications, CDs need further modification to suppress these limitations. In this way, the utilization of suitable porous support in CD-based fluorimetric sensors is believed to be a promising strategy for synthesizing a high-performance sensing material for the detection of L-AA [23,24]. Notably, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), including crystalline materials with many pores, high flexibility, and superior specific surface area, have emerged as a new platform for the synthesis of sensing materials [25]. In addition, the different types of MOFs with a variety of buildings, and cavity sizes [26] have been showing a high potential for a wide range of applications in gas adsorption [27], catalytic activity [28], drug delivery [29], and many other applications [30]. MOF-199 has emerged as the newest class with a three-dimensional structure consisting of poor coordination between Cu2+ metal with benzene-1,3,5-tri carboxylic acid ligand [31]. MOF-199 has been widely used in the separation, and adsorption of materials due to its special structure, sustainability, and thermal durability [32,33]. Considering the characteristics of high surface area and pore volume of MOF-199, the superior accumulation of analyte on the probe surface and the amplification of the sensing signal for the guest CD can occur. The selectivity and sensitivity of sensor significantly improve via encapsulating CDs into the cavities of MOF-199 [34]. Despite the excellent properties of MOF-199 as an ideal host, few optical sensors based on MOF-199 have been reported.

In this research work, we used orange peels waste and ethylene diamine as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, for a cost-effective, non-toxic, and ecologically acceptable preparation of blue N-CDs [35]. In the past, citric acid was used as one of the sources of CDs [36]. Recently it has been found that using agro-waste biomass can be a sustainable alternative route for the synthesis of valuable fluorescent CDs, functionalizing the surface without using chemicals materials, and managing biomass waste in order to reduce environmental pollution [37,38]. Biomass refers to waste from agricultural and food, fruit peel, and animal, which is available at low cost and provides biodegradable, and renewable carbon source [39,40]. The CDs--derived from biomass can be an attractive and non-expensive way to prepare fluorimetric sensor for determination of L-AA. The blue CDs were capped with ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a chelating ligand to prepare N-CD@EDTA and then, it was embedded into the MOF-199 structure. As a result, a selective and efficient fluorescence sensor based on N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199 (N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199) was constructed for L-AA. Herein, the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 has well-resolved emission peak at 415 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 310 nm; while it strongly enhances in the presence of L-AA. The sensor can measure low level of L-AA concentration with a suitable and wide linear range (10 nM −100 μM), high sensitivity (0.201μM−1), and low limit of detection of 8.6 nM. It is confirmed that N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 shows superior sensing performance toward L-AA than control samples (N-CD@EDTA, MOF-199, and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199) which demonstrates the vital role of each component in overall sensing process. In the following, the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (L-AA) further be applied to detect Fe3+ ions via on-off signal fluorescence intensity. Iron ion (Fe3+) is one of the elements that are essential in processes such as cell metabolism and electron transfer [41]. On the other hand, its deficiency causes various diseases [42]. Therefore, we need to achieve a simple, inexpensive, and accessible method for measuring Fe3+ in several examples.

Every year, a large amount of orange peels is thrown away without any use as waste. Finding the right solution to turn orange peels into high-value materials can be a good way to face this problem. In this work, orange peels as carbon precursor were used to synthesize CDs by using hydrothermal method in high temperature. On the other hand, MOF-199 has received a lot of attention in recent years and has been used in many applications, including as an adsorbent and catalyst support, etc. However, limited works have been reported on the application of this material in fluorescence sensors. This study could be regarded as a new pathway, combining the characteristics of MOF-199 and specific optical properties of N-CD@EDTA towards consecutively quantitative analysis of L-AA and Fe3+ ions based on fluorescent probes synthesized from agro-waste biomass. In addition, the removal efficiency of L-AA in an aqueous solution by the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was studied, and the adsorption isotherms and kinetics of process were investigated. Due to the high surface area and porosity architecture of the nanohybrid, L-AA molecules were enriched inside or onto the surface of the framework. The calculated surface adsorption capacity of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 verified its superior performance for simultaneous determination and removal of L-AA.

Copper (II) nitrate trihydrate, benzene1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (BTC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF 99.5 %), dichloromethane (99.8 %), ethanol (96 %), disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, ethylene diamine, L-AA, iron (III) chloride anhydrous, and EDTA were purchase from Merck. For the synthesis of CDs, orange peels were purchased from a local fruit market (Mazandaran province, Iran).

The structures of pristine and modified MOF-199 were identified by Bruker D8 X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation. A double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco V 750) was utilized to acquire absorption spectra. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using Jasco FP 8300 spectrofluorometer. Thermo Nicolet AVATAR was used to record Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. The morphology and structure of samples were investigated via field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, MIRA3TESCAN-XMU) and, further with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips EM 208S). Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) and mapping elemental analysis were performed using a MIRA3 TESCAN-XMU. The porosity of the material was measured with N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (BELSRP mini II). Moreover, CHN analysis was performed on dried orange peels using PERKIN ELMER SERIES II 2400. Atomic absorption spectroscopy (Analytik Jena) was used to evaluate the validity of method to determine the concentration of Fe3+ in real samples.

Orange peels was used as a biomass source for the synthesis of N-CD@EDTA. After washing and drying the sample in an oven, the peels were completely powdered with a mortar and pestle. 4 g of washed orange peels was placed in porcelain crucible and heated in vacuum oven at 70 °C for 6 h, so the sample weight was reached to 1.3 g. The water content of orange peels was measured about 67.5 %. CHN analysis on dried orange peels showed the presence of hydrogen (5.21 %) and carbon (40.64 %) while no nitrogen was found.

For synthesis of N-CD@EDTA, 25 mg of powdered orange peels, 40 mL of deionized water and 1.25 mL of ethylene diamine were sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave equipped with a 100 mL Teflon and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. Then, it was naturally cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 7500 rpm to remove gross particles. The transparent solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane. To improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor, the surface of the N-CDs nanoparticles was modified. In this regard, 50 mM EDTA (25 mL) was added into 4 mL N-CDs solution, and the mixture was refluxed at 60 °C for 24 h. The resultant was collected as N-CD@EDTA and stored at 4 °C for further usage [43,44].

For preparation of Cu-based MOF (MOF-199), 5 g of CuNO3.3H2O and 0.25 g of BTC were added into the mixture of solvents including deionized water (4.25 mL), ethanol (4.25 mL), and DMF (4.25 mL) followed by stirring for 10 min [45]. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 85 °C for 24 h. Finally, the blue precipitate was collected via centrifuging and washed with ethanol, DMF, and dichloromethane three times. The obtained powder was dried at 60 °C for 24 h and then collected.

In order to encapsulate N-CD@EDTA into the structure of MOF-199, the “bottle around the ship” method was used [34]. Various volumes of N-CD@EDTA (4, 8, 13, 18, 25 mL) were mixed with deionized water (4.25 mL), ethanol (4.25 mL), and DMF (4.25 mL), separately and stirred for 10 min. Afterward, they were poured into the mixture of 0.5 g of CuNO3.3H2O and 0.25 g of BTC, followed by stirring for 15 min. The mixtures were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and the procedure was performed according to the synthesis method of MOF-199. The hybrids are denoted N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (x); where x indicates the used volume of N-CD@EDTA in the process. The schematic illustration for the synthesis of the probe is given in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for ON-OFF fluorimetric determination
ce:section.ce:para:
5 mg of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was weighed, then dissolved in 5 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH: 8) and sonicated for 5 min to form a colloidal solution. 3 mL of preparedsolution was poured into the cuvette, and then, the fluorescence spectra toward the addition of different L-AA concentrations were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The band widths are set as 10:20 for the excitation and emission, respectively. In order to ensure the reliability of the experimental results, all the fluorescent measurements were repeated five times. To measure the fluorescent QY of N-CDs, quinine sulfate solution (in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as a reference material. The QY of N-CDs was calculated according to eq. (1):
(1) Qc = Qs × FC AS (ŋC)2/ FS AC (ŋS)2

Qc (QY CD), Qs (QY quinine sulfate = 54 %), FC, and FS are the integral areas of fluorescent emission spectra of N-CDs and quinine sulfate with an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, respectively, Ac, and As represent the absorbance intensity of N-CDs and quinine sulfate, ŋc and ŋs account for a reflective index of N-CDs and quinine sulfate solutions. To minimize the resorption effects, the absorption intensity of N-CDs and quinine sulfate solutions was kept below 0.1 [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for determination of L-AA and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the real sample, the orange juice was purchased from a local supermarket. 15 mL of juice was transferred into a falcon tube and centrifuged at 7500 rpm for about 30 min. The supernatant solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and 0.5 mL of the solution was diluted with 50 mL of PBS (pH = 8). Then 5 mg of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was dissolved in 5 mL of the above solution and subjected to an ultrasound for 10 min. Different concentrations of L-AA solution were added to the sample, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. (The orange juice information is given in Table S1).

Quantification of L-AA was performed using a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Waters) instrument equipped with a dual UV-Vis detector. At room temperature the quantification of L-AA was performed on a reversed phase C18 column (25 mm × 4.6 mm I.D., 5 μm particle size). The mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile and water (70:30). The analyte was eluted by an isocratic program at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min −1. The UV detector was set at the wavelenght of 265 nm.

For the water samples, 15 mL of river water (Babolrood in Mazandaran province) and tap water were taken. After 30 min of centrifugation, they were filtered to remove impurities and diluted 50:50 with PBS (pH = 8). Then, 5 mg of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was added to the 5 mL of the diluted samples. To evaluate the capability of the sensor to measure Fe3 + ions in real samples via an on-off signal, L-AA (100 μM in the final solution) was added. Then, different concentrations of Fe3 + ions were spiked. The initial Fe3+ ion content in river water was found to be zero via atomic adsorption. The relative recovery was employed in the analysis of real samples and can be calculated as follows:
(2)
Recovery =

In order to measure the point of zero charge (pHpzc), 40 mL of 0.1 M NaNO3 solution was added to ten erlenmeyer flasks. Their pH was adjusted to 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12 by NaOH and HNO3 (0.1 M). After that, 5 mg of adsorbent was added to all the solutions and placed on the shaker for 24 h. After this time, the pH was read by apH meter. The secondary pH was subtracted from the primary, the curve of pH-pH0 versus pH0 was plotted.

For adsorption studies, a stock solution of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (35.22 mg L−1) was prepared by dissolving N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (1 mg) in distilled water (5 mL). The kinetics of the surface adsorption process was investigated by changing the contact time parameter (0–60 min) between the adsorbent and L-AA in optimized conditions (pH = 9 and dose of L-AA = 35.22 mg L−1). Surface adsorption isotherm was achieved by changing the initial dose of adsorbate (L-AA: 5.28, 8.8, 14.08, 17.61, 26.41, 35.22 mg L−1) in the constant pH = 9 and time of 1 min, respectively, and is expressed in Langmuir and Freundlich models.

The preliminary evidence for the formation of the MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 samples were obtained by XRD technique (Fig. 1 A). The XRD pattern of the N-CD@EDTA sample shows a broad peak at 2θ: 20.76°, which corresponds to the amorphous carbon obtained from orange peels.The presence of (200), (220), (222), (420), (331), (422), (511), (440), (600), (444), (551), (731), (751), (773) and (882) planes of the pyramidal structure of MOF-199 lattice were confirmed via the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 6.76, 9.52, 11.64, 13.48, 14.3, 16.48, 17.7, 19.06, 20.3, 23.05, 24.25, 26.28, 29.4, 35.15 and 39.15○ [47,48]. The XRD pattern of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 is similar to the pattern of MOF-199. It can be concluded that the introduction of fluorescent N-CD@EDTA can not affect the crystal structure of MOF-199. On the other hand, no peaks related to N-CD@EDTA can be observed due to its low concentration or small size of the guest species.

To study any interaction that occurred between MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA, FT-IR analysis was performed and presented in Fig. 1B. As shown, the FTIR spectrum of MOF-199 shows several typical absorption bands, such as the bands at 1372, 1446, and 1644 cm−1, which are attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the carboxylate (COO−) groups, respectively. In addition, the bands at 760 and 489 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of Cu–O in Cu-based MOFs. The broad band at 3440 cm−1 is characteristic of O–H bonds of water molecules. Also, the several peaks in the 700-1300 cm−1 region belong to the out-of-plane vibrations of benzene [33,47]. For N-CD@EDTA, the peaks at 1399, 1638, and 3450 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of C–N, CO, and O–H groups, respectively, which are assigned to the functionalization of N-CD [43]. All of these bands can also be observed in MOF modified by N-CD@EDTA, and no significant change in the FTIR spectrum of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 can be observed.

FESEM images of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 are presented in Fig. 1C and D, respectively. Some large crystals are formed with pyramidal morphology and distinct edges and have relatively rough surfaces. Others have irregular shape with different dimensions formed beneath the larger crystals. The images show that no significant changes in the morphology of MOF-199 were occurred after modification by N-CD@EDTA. TEM images of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 1E and F) show uniform distribution of spherical N-CDs throughout the sample which indicate the successful encapsulation of N-CD@EDTA into the structure of MOF-199. The measured diameter of each CD is well below 10 nm (Fig. 1E and F).

Elemental analysis on N-CD@EDTA, MOF-199, and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was performed to monitor the presence of different elements in the samples. The Kα lines of O, C, and N appear in EDS spectrum of N-CD@EDTA (Fig. 2 A). In Fig. 2B, EDS spectrum of MOF-199 is presented which contains the characteristic X-ray lines of O, C and Cu elements. With introducing the N-CD@EDTA into MOF-199, the N Kα line appears in the spectrum of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 2C). On the other hand, elemental mapping approves the homogenous dispersion of O, C, N, and Cu in the selected area of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 2D and E). Weight percentage of existing elements in the samples is presented in Table 1 .

Geometrical properties such as specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore diameter were analyzed through N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of MOF-199, and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 and their corresponding plots of pore size distributions. According to Fig. 3 A and B, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 show the type I and classified in the category of microporous materials (<2 nm). Meantime, no hysteresis loop is observed in none of the adsorption-desorption, which indicates the formation of homogenous pores in the structure [48,49]. Table 2 shows a summary of the obtained results derived from Brunauer -Emmett –Teller (BET) and Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) plots. The specific surface area of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 are calculated to be 948.43 and 1240.7 m2 g−1, respectively. The nanohybrid has acquired a higher surface area after the introduction of N-CD@EDTA, which makes the sensor more susceptible to adsorption and removal of L-AA. BJH plots of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 3C and D) show the formation of microporous structure with mean pore size diameter around 1.2 nm.

The optical properties of N-CD@EDTA, MOF-199, and also N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 were, firstly, evaluated via UV–Vis spectrometer. As can be seen in Fig. 4 A, N-CD@EDTA displays two distinct peaks at around 230 and 280 nm in the spectrum, which corresponds to π -π* and n -π*electron transfers of CC and CO bonds, respectively [50,51]. In the spectrum of MOF-199, one adsorption peak at 230 nm can be assigned to the π-π* transfer of the Cu-oxo cluster in Cu-MOF [51]. For N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199, it has the characteristic absorption spectra of both N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199. A little shift for the n-π* electron transfer of the CO bond is observed, which may be due to the tendency of functional groups on CDs for selective combination with Cu nodes [52].

The photo-stability tests of N-CD@EDTA in buffer solution over five days indicate the repeatable and stable emission of N-CD@EDTA (Fig. 4B) which can be embedded as suitable fluorophore in the MOF-199 matrix. The bright blue fluorescence can be observed from N-CD@EDTA under UV lamp at 365 nm (inset Fig. 4B). The effect of increasing the excitation wavelength on the emission peak position was investigated and the spectra showed the red shift in fluorescence wavelengths (Fig. 4C). The reasons for such behavior may be due to the non-homogeneous size of N-CD@EDTA nanoparticles or their different surface states [44]. In addition, the QY of N-CD@EDTA was calculated as 3.61 %.

The fluorescence spectra of the N-CD@EDTA, MOF-199, and N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 recorded under excitation wavelength of 310 nm are shown in Fig. 4D. The emission of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 appears at 410 nm, and the photographic images under irradiation of 365 nm shows the blue fluorescence for N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199, which demonstrates the successful loading of N-CD@EDTA (inset of Fig. 4D). After encapsulating N-CD@EDTA in MOF-199, a decrease along with a red shift in the emission of N-CD@EDTA occurred. Usually, metal ions such as Fe2 + and Cu2 + can cause to the static quenching of the CD emission. So it can be concluded that the weak emission of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 can be raised through the interaction of Cu (II) with the nitrogen or OH functional groups on the N-CD, which results in electron transfer from the photo-excited CD to copper [53]:
(3) CDs + Cu(II) + BTC →CDs@Cu - MOF

In order to evaluate the role of different materials in the fluorescent probe, a series of experiments for L-AA determination was designed using N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199 as control samples and the results are compared to that for N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 5 A). As can be seen, the fluorescent intensity of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 enhances in the presence of L-AA (50 μM), while such behavior cannot be observed for N-CD and MOF-199. So, CD@EDTA/MOF-199 can be used as sensitive turn-on probe for the sensing of L-AA. Anyway, each of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA play important roles in sensing performance of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199. By integrating the excellent optical properties of N-CD@EDTA and the superior adsorption capability of MOF-199 owing to its high external surface area, the sensor with enhanced performance for L-AA determination is constructed.

Herein, a series of experiments were conducted for further exploring the possible mechanism of fluorescence enhancment induced by L-AA. As shown in Fig. 5B, the XRD patterns of the fresh N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 and after treatment with L-AA are a little different. The intensities of some diffraction peaks of MOF-199 at 2θ of 6.76, 9.52, 11.64, and 13.48° have decreased or disappeared while some new peaks are appeared. In FT-IR spectra of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (Fig. 5C), the peaks in 700–1200 cm−1 have changed and new peaks are appeared. It seems that the partial changes of the MOF-199 crystals may be occurred during the detection of L-AA by N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199.

L-AA with enediol group in its structure has strong reduction ability and can be easily oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) in the presence of chemical reducer species. In the presence of L-AA, the Cu (II) ions in the clusters of MOF-199 are reduced to Cu (I) ions, so the encapsulated N-CD@EDTA nanoparticles are released, and the fluorescence can be recovered.

The recognition mechanism is attributed to the contribution of both of MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA. The fluorescent N-CD@EDTA probe with small particle size and photo-stability serves as suitable transducer in the proposed sensor. MOF-199 with high surface area and porosity as a superior adsorbent is effective in enriching more L-AA molecules onto the surface of the probe and also hindering the accumulation of N-CD@EDTA.

The removal performance of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 towards L-AA was further investigated. At first, the absorption spectrum of L-AA (35.22 mg L−1) was recorded in the range of 190–700 nm. As shown in Fig. 5D, the absorption spectrum of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (7 mg L−1) in buffer solution (pH = 9) has a maximum peak at 270 nm which is suppressed after adsorption of L-AA. Fig. S1A indicates the calibration plot of the L-AA in accordance with its absorption in the presence of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199. In the following, the effective parameters on L-AA removal from solution using N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 were studied. The adsorption capacity (qe) which indicates the mg of adsorbate per adsorbent (g), can be determined via equation (4):
(4)

c e
)
V m
Where, V and m present the volume of solution (L) and adsorbent amount (g), respectively.

Firstly, the effect of contact time between N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 and L-AA was investigated in the range of 0–60 min (Fig. S1B). For this aim, a specific buffer solution of L-AA (50 μM) in the presence of 7 mg L−1 of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 was prepared, and the spectrum was taken from the upper solution during specific times. It can be seen that the qe is obtained at its highest value in 10 min. In lower time, the large number of available vacant adsorption sites may not be fully occupied and is reached after 10 min by decreasing the number of available sites. In addition, the effect of other parameters including pH, dose of adsorbent, initial dose of L-AA, and the temperature are investigated onto the qe and plotted in Fig. S1 C, D, E, and F, respectively. As can be seen, the optimized parameters are obtained at pH = 9, dose of adsorbent = 7 mg L−1, initial dose of L-AA = 35.22 mg L−1, and temperature of 25 °C. Meantime, The qe in the optimum condition (qmax) is calculated to be 4.61 mg g−1.

In general, the pseudo-first-order model describes the physical adsorption of adsorbate on adsorbent, and the chemisorption behavior can be well described by the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. Fig. 6 A and B shows the linear plots for the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models based on equations (6) and (7), respectively:

where qt (mg g−1) mentions to adsorption capacity at time t (min). In addition, the rate constants of pseudo-first/second order models are defined with k1 (min−1) and k2 (g min−1 mg−1), respectively. The parameters of kinetic models are determined from the equations and presented in Table 3 . The coefficient of determination (R2) in the pseudo-second-order kinetic model is more favorable than the pseudo-first-order kinetic. It can be concluded that the pseudo-second-order model has a greater ability to express the rate of the adsorption process.

The surface properties and adsorption capacities of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 can be expressed via the adsorption isotherms model. The influence of the initial concentration of the L-AA on its adsorption value has been analyzed using equations (7) and (8).

Where KL (mg g−1) and qmax (mg g−1) are Langmuir constant and equilibrium adsorption capacity, KF (L mg−1) and n are Freundlich model parameters.

The parameters of the models were calculated by plotting the corresponding equations (Fig. 6C and D) and presented in Table 4 . Based on the results, Langmuir isotherm offered a more logical model owing to its R2 value. This confirms the monolayer coverage of L-AA and the homogeneous distribution of active sites on the adsorbent. In order to determine the compatibility of the adsorption process by Langmuir isotherm, the separation factor (RL) was also calculated to be 0.072 in accordance to equation (9):
(9)

It is known that, with dissociation constants of pKa1 = 4.17 and pKa2 = 11.58, L-AA may be present in the non-dissociated (L-AA), singly deprotonated (L-AA-) and doubly deprotonated (L-AA2-) forms in different pH values [54]. On the other hand, the surface charge of the adsorbent is an important factor in the adsorption process, which changes with solution pH [55]. Fig. S2 shows the graph of ΔpH vs. of pH0. According to the figure, it can be seen that the pHpzc of the adsorbent is equal to 5.5. Therefore, at the optimum pH = 9, the surface charges of the adsorbents would be negative. On the other hand, in the optimum pH = 9, the main species present in the medium is L-AA-. At pH > pH optimum, the doubly negative charged L-AA (L-AA2-) are more difficult to adsorb on the negatively charged surface of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199. This led to further decrease in the adsorption of L-AA owing to the creation of electrostatic repulsion force. It seems that at the optimum pH, the adsorption of L-AA over N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 may be governed by π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding rather than the electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of L-AA sensing via fluorimetric measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The process of optimizing the parameters such as pH, dose, and incubation time, which affect the performance of the probe, was carried out to achieve maximum sensitivity and precision (Fig. 6E). Initially, the probe was used for the fluorimetric determination of L-AA (50 μM) in different pH of PBS at constant incubation time. The results show that at very low or high pH, the values of F/F0 decrease. This phenomenon can be expressed by lower affinity of L-AA to the binding sites of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 at high concentrations of H+ or OH−, and therefore, a significant decrease in F/F0 may occur. In addition, the protonation and deprotonation of the N-CD functional groups in highly acidic or basic environments can be another reason.

N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 probe was used to investigate the effect of interval times for L- L-AA measurements. As can be seen in Fig. 6F, a sharp increase in F/F0 values is observed up to 3 min immediately after adding L-AA, indicating a short response time for the interaction between L-AA and probe. At longer times, it becomes almost constant, indicating saturation of binding sites for L-AA. Considering time-saving and the high F/F0 value, other studies were performed after 3 min of incubation at pH = 9.

In order to evaluate the analytical performance of sensor, some N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (x) were prepared through addition of different amount of N-CD@EDTA (4, 8, 13, 18, 25 mL) to MOF-199 precursors solution and the performance of sensors were investigated toward L-AA. The changes in F/F0 ratios of sensors with addition of L-AA were studied under optimal conditions and the calibration curves are plotted for each probe (Fig. 7 and S3). The results show that CD@EDTA/MOF-199 with the content of 13 mL N-CD@EDTA (N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (13)) has the highest slope (0.201 µM-1) and sensitivity toward L-AA (Fig. 7 A) and determine L-AA in lower concentrations range (10 nM - 100 μM) than other probes. The LOD corresponds to 3Sb/m, where Sb and m are the blank standard deviations, and the slope of calibration curve, is calculated to be 8.6 nM. In addition, N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (13) has higher sensitivity than N-CD/MOF-199 (13) which may be related to the presence of EDTA chelating agents to donate electrons and form coordination complexes with L-AA (Fig. 7B). The photographic images of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (13) sensor excited at 365 nm were recorded after addition of different concentrations of L-AA which display the increase in emission intensity with analyte concentration (Fig. 7C). The results indicate the outstanding potential of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 (13) for determination of L-AA in trace level. Table 5 compares the analytical performance of the proposed probe with other works. It can be seen that N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 possesses lower LOD and broader linear range in comparison with other sensors.

The repeatability of the sensor was considered as inter-day item. The fluorescent intensity of the probe in the presence of 50 μM L-AA, after five measurements during one day (inter-day), was recorded (Fig. 8 A). In addition, the reproducibility experiment was performed on six fresh N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 probes prepared similarly. The results presented in Fig. 8B indicate that no significant difference in their responses toward L-AA is observed. Also, the result of long-term stability of probe after 40 days for the sensing performance (Fig. 8C) indicates near accordance with the obtained F/F0 on first day. These results confirmed that the proposed probe has a stable response, which can afford credible measurements in a complicated matrix toward L-AA.

Selectivity is a vital feature in evaluating the applicability of probes in complicated matrix such as biological samples. Therefore, to evaluate the anti-interference ability and selectivity of the probe toward L-AA determination from different biological molecules, 50 μM of Fructose (Fruc), Glutamine (Glut), Glycine (Gly), l-cysteine (L-Cy), Maltose (Mal), Urea (Ur), Ethanol (Et), Glycerol (Glyc), Citric Acid (CA) were added to the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 probe solution (Fig. 9 A). The results showed no remarkable turn-on signal occurred for biological molecules, but in the presence of L-AA, the F/F0 value significantly increased. On the other hand, the anti-interference ability of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 probe was investigated in the presence of the same concentrations of L-AA and biological molecules (Fig. 9B), and the results showed that the response of the probe to L-AA even in the presence of biological species shows no difference. Therefore, it can be concluded that the presence of biological species makes negligible interfering effects on the probe response toward L-AA, indicating the selective and anti-interference feature of the proposed probe. The integrating features of N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199 in N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 provide the outstanding selectivity of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199. Notably, two probes of N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199 show no specific response toward L-AA addition (Fig. 9C and E, respectively); they have even lower sensitivity to other molecules such as leucine than N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199. Also, it seems that, their F/F0 toward L-AA can be affected by the interactions of other species (Fig. 9D and F). So, the weak capability for selective determination of L-AA can be acquired for N-CD@EDTA and MOF-199.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A real sample, including orange juice, was used to assess the applicability of the prepared sensor. After the pretreatment of the samples according to section 2.6, the standard addition method was performed and the results are recorded (Fig. S4A). From the calibration curve (Fig. S4B), the LOD and sensitivity are calculated to be 2.37 μM and 0.0944 μM−1, respectively.

The same sets of experiments were carried out using HPLC for different concentrations of L-AA (30, 80, and 100 μM) to verify the reliability of the proposed method for analyzing orange juice sample. The obtained results from the HPLC technique are compared with that of the proposed method (Table 6 ). As can be seen, the obtained recovery values by the fluorescence method are in consistent with the HPLC recoveries. The consistency of results confirms that the present method can be considered as a reliable alternative to conventional methods for the quantitative determination of L-AA in foods.

N-CD@EDTA with oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl has affinity to Fe3+ and act as fluorophore in N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 nanohybrid for its “on-off” fluorimetric determination [56]. Owing to the encapsulating of N-CD@EDTA into the MOF-199 structure and quenching its emission, determination of Fe3+ is impossible. So, N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) with strong enhanced emission was used for measurement of Fe3+ concentration. By adding the consecutive concentrations of Fe3+ to the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) solution, an obvious fluorescence quenching occurred (Fig. 10 A). The linear response (F0/F) of the N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) to Fe3+ concentration was observed in the range of 100–650 μM with the calibration equation of Y = 0.005 X+0.4383 and a good linear correlation coefficeint (R2 = 0.9913) (Fig. 10B). The LOD and sensitivity (S/N = 3) are obtained as 1.15 μM and 0.005 μM−1, respectively. The results show the enough potential of the proposed N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) as a "quenched" fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ determination. Fig. 10C exhibits the photographic images of CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) system after addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. As can be seen, the blue emission intensity decreases with increasing the ion concentrations.

Regarding to the sensing mechanism of Fe3+, the static quenching of the fluorescence of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) system is found owing to a red shift and extended band at 210 nm (Fig. 10D). The fluorescence quenching may be attributed to transport of electrons from excited states of CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) to unoccupied orbitals of Fe3+ ions. Also, there is no overlap between the wavelengths of CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) emission and Fe3+ absorbance. So, this decreases the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) while the quenching process takes place [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3 + in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples (tap and river water) were selected to investigate the application and feasibility of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM) to determine Fe3+. Before performing the relevant tests, the amount of Fe3+ ions is measred via atomic absorption spectroscopy and estimated to be zero. Different volume of Fe3 + standard solutions was spiked to the samples prepared in section 2.6, and related fluorescent spectra were recorded (Fig. 11 A and C). The calibration curves for tap and river water are plotted (Fig. 11B and D) and related LOD values are calculated to be 61.48 and 30.61 μM, respectively. Also, the good recoveries for water samples are almost in agreement to the results obtained via atomic absorption spectroscopy; demonstrating the validity of the proposed technique (Table 7 ) and the applicability of the sensor in the complicated media.

In summary, the EDTA-capped CDs as fluorescence probe was embedded into the microporous structure of MOF-199 and employed for quantitative analysis of L-AA and Fe3+ ions at trace level. The characterizations of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199 by TEM, FESEM, EDS, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy indicate the successful encapsulating of EDTA-capped CDs into the MOF-199 structure. The proposed sensor shows a highly sensitive and selective response for L-AA determination, whereas the pristine MOF-199 and N-CD@EDTA did not show any signal to the addition of L-AA, proving the successful rational design of the L-AA fluorimetric assay. The set of optimization experiments determined the fluorescence assay condition of pH = 8, the volume of N-CD@EDTA of 13 mL and the short incubation time of 3 min. Taking advantage of great host capability of MOF-199 for its large specific surface area as well as the porosity virtues and the favorable fluorescent properties of the guest N-CDs@EDTA, not only can recognize L-AA with a wide sensitive range (10 nM-100 μM) and low LOD, but also exhibit high adsorption capacity for removal of L-AA (4.61 mg. g− 1). The obtained results confirm that the proposed sensor has good reusability, stability for determination of L-AA in orange juice. Fe3 + ions were further measured based on fluorescence quenching of N-CD@EDTA/MOF-199-AA (100 μM). The sensor has high recovery for Fe3+ in water samples and present wide linear range and LOD in micomolar range.
